skill.name: entra-app-registration
skill.description: Guides Microsoft Entra ID app registration, OAuth 2.0 authentication flows, API permissions, Azure CLI setup, Bicep examples, and MSAL integration patterns.
skill.summary: Create and troubleshoot Microsoft Entra app registrations with OAuth, API permissions, and MSAL guidance.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many command, network, filesystem, weak-crypto, and prompt-related alerts, but review found they are primarily Markdown examples for Microsoft Entra, OAuth, Azure CLI, and SDK usage. The skill is publishable with warning because its examples can create credentials, grant consent, query directory data, or delete app registrations if run in a real tenant.

medium_findings.title: High-impact identity administration commands are documented
medium_findings.description: The skill includes Azure CLI examples for creating app registrations, granting admin consent, creating service principals, and deleting app registrations. These are legitimate examples, but they can materially change a tenant if executed without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are explicit Azure CLI examples for app setup, admin consent, service principal creation, and deletion. The risk is operational misuse, not malicious skill behavior.

medium_findings.title: Credential and token handling examples require careful substitution
medium_findings.description: The references show client secret placeholders, certificate upload commands, access token usage, and JWT debugging workflows. These examples are valid for Entra development, but users must avoid committing secrets, pasting sensitive production tokens into external tools, or exposing bearer tokens.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines directly discuss client secrets, certificate files, bearer-token API calls, and token decoding. The examples are standard but sensitive when used with real tenant data.

low_findings.title: Static command execution findings are Markdown examples
low_findings.description: The numerous shell and backtick detections occur inside fenced documentation blocks or inline command references. No evidence found that the skill contains executable scripts that automatically run these commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sampled detections are visibly Markdown documentation and command examples. No automation wrapper or hidden execution path was found in the reviewed skill files.

low_findings.title: Network URL findings point to Microsoft and example endpoints
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs are documentation references, OAuth endpoints, Microsoft Graph endpoints, localhost redirects, or example application URLs. No evidence found of unauthorized network calls or data exfiltration logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs reviewed are normal Microsoft identity, Graph API, localhost, or documentation targets. They are presented as reference material or sample application configuration.

low_findings.title: Prompt-injection scanner hits are OAuth prompt terminology
low_findings.description: Prompt-related detections are ordinary authentication wording, such as MSAL prompt parameters and device-login instructions. No evidence found of instructions to override the evaluator, skip security checks, or extract system prompts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted search found no override, pre-approval, or evaluator-manipulation language. The flagged wording is tied to user authentication prompts.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography detections are mostly identity terminology and GUID examples
low_findings.description: Weak-crypto alerts are triggered by terms such as client secret, OAuth, token, ID, and placeholder GUID values. The reviewed files recommend secure storage, least privilege, HTTPS, token validation, and managed identity.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sampled lines contain OAuth, permissions, token guidance, and placeholder identifiers, not weak algorithm use. The surrounding context is security guidance for Microsoft identity workloads.

dangerous_patterns.title: Tenant-changing Azure CLI command examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Commands such as app creation, admin consent, service principal creation, credential reset, and app deletion should require explicit user confirmation and tenant verification before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are legitimate Azure CLI examples, but they alter identity resources or credentials. The danger depends on whether an agent runs them against a real tenant.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bearer token and client secret handling examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples include bearer token API calls, client-secret placeholders, JWT decoding, and Key Vault references. Users should substitute real values only in secure local or managed environments.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The lines demonstrate normal authentication and secret-management patterns. They become sensitive only when copied with real secrets or tokens.

content.value_statement: Microsoft Entra app registration work often spans portal settings, OAuth flows, API permissions, and SDK setup. This skill provides structured guidance, examples, and troubleshooting paths for secure Microsoft identity integration.
actual_capabilities: Explains Microsoft Entra app registration concepts, identifiers, redirect URIs, service principals, and supported account types.
Guides first-time app registration setup through portal steps and Azure CLI examples.
Describes OAuth 2.0 authorization code, client credentials, device code, and implicit flow patterns.
Maps delegated and application Microsoft Graph permissions to consent and least-privilege decisions.
Provides MSAL examples for console applications and SDK quick references for common Azure identity libraries.
Helps troubleshoot redirect URI, consent, token, client secret, and application configuration errors.
limitations: It does not verify the current state of a tenant or app registration unless connected tooling provides that data.
It cannot grant consent, create secrets, or change Azure resources without separate user-approved execution.
It provides examples that must be adapted to each tenant, app type, and security policy.
It is not a replacement for organization-specific identity governance or compliance review.

use_cases.title: Create a first app registration
use_cases.description: Walk through app type selection, redirect URI setup, client ID capture, and initial Microsoft Graph permissions.

use_cases.title: Standardize service authentication
use_cases.description: Plan client credentials flow, certificate or secret usage, service principal creation, and least-privilege application permissions.

use_cases.title: Diagnose sign-in and consent failures
use_cases.description: Trace redirect URI mismatches, missing consent, token claim issues, and invalid client secret errors.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a new registration
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a Microsoft Entra app registration for a web application. Ask for the required details before suggesting settings.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Graph permissions
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my app requirements and recommend delegated or application Microsoft Graph permissions with least privilege.

prompt_templates.title: Implement an OAuth flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Show the correct OAuth 2.0 flow and MSAL setup for a console app that signs in users and calls Microsoft Graph.

prompt_templates.title: Audit and troubleshoot configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this Entra app registration configuration and identify redirect URI, consent, secret, token, and tenant risks.

output_examples.input: I need a single-tenant web app registration for an internal dashboard.
output: Recommended sign-in audience: accounts in this organizational directory only.
Register web redirect URIs exactly as used by the application.
Use authorization code flow with MSAL and store client credentials outside source control.
Request only the Microsoft Graph scopes needed by the dashboard.

output_examples.input: My app fails with AADSTS50011 after sign-in.
output: Compare the request redirect URI with the registered URI exactly.
Check scheme, host casing, path, trailing slash, port, and platform type.
Update the app registration or application configuration so the values match.

output_examples.input: A background service must call Microsoft Graph without a user.
output: Use client credentials flow with application permissions.
Prefer certificate or managed identity where possible.
Grant admin consent only for the minimal required permissions.
Rotate credentials and monitor service principal sign-ins.

best_practices: Confirm tenant, app ID, and redirect URI values before running any Azure CLI command.
Use least-privilege permissions and document why each permission is required.
Store secrets in managed identity, Key Vault, or secure environment configuration instead of source files.
anti_patterns: Granting broad application permissions before validating the exact business requirement.
Copying placeholder client secrets or tenant IDs into shared repositories or tickets.
Running cleanup or credential reset commands against production without an explicit rollback plan.

faq.question: Can this skill create an app registration for me?
faq.answer: It can guide the steps and provide commands, but execution requires separate Azure access and user approval.

faq.question: Does it support both Azure AD and Microsoft Entra naming?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains Microsoft Entra ID and references Azure AD terminology where the tooling still uses it.

faq.question: Can it help choose delegated versus application permissions?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains both permission types and recommends least-privilege choices based on the app scenario.

faq.question: Does it include MSAL examples?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes console app examples and SDK quick references for common Microsoft identity libraries.

faq.question: Is it safe to paste real client secrets into prompts?
faq.answer: No. Use placeholders in prompts and keep real secrets in approved secret stores or local secure configuration.

faq.question: Can it troubleshoot AADSTS errors?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers common redirect URI, consent, tenant, client secret, and token-related errors.
